query: symptoms of tardive dyskinesia in patients on long-term antipsychotic therapy

retrieved_docs.title: Tardive Dyskinesia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Tardive dyskinesia is a syndrome that includes a group of iatrogenic movement disorders caused by the blockade of dopamine receptors. The movement disorders include akathisia, dystonia, buccolingual stereotypy, myoclonus, chorea, tics, and other abnormal involuntary movements, which are commonly caused by the long-term use of typical antipsychotics. However, several other medications are also associated with tardive dyskinesia. The Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, Fifth Edition (DSM V) defines tardive dyskinesia as a medication-induced movement disorder that persists despite discontinuation or change of the medications. As per DSM-V, to confirm a diagnosis of tardive dyskinesia, symptoms must persist for a month after discontinuation of the drug. This activity reviews tardive dyskinesia and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in recognizing and managing patients affected by this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Tardive Dyskinesia -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Other factors increasing tardive dyskinesia diathesis include the duration of exposure to the neuroleptic agent, the manifestation of EPS, and using first-generation antipsychotics instead of second-generation antipsychotics. Of note, the long-term use of anticholinergic medications may increase the risk of tardive dyskinesia.

retrieved_docs.title: Extrapyramidal Side Effects -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Acute dystonic reactions are typically transient, but in late-onset and persistent tardive dystonia, symptoms can persist for years. [58] A study involving 107 cases of tardive dystonia reported that only 14% of patients achieved remission over a mean follow-up period of 8.5 years. [59] Acute akathisia may spontaneously resolve or improve with appropriate medication; however, there are reported cases of tardive akathisia persisting over many years. [60] Tardive dyskinesia may be unable to be reversed, with a cumulative persistence rate as high as 82% in a study involving patients with schizophrenia. [61]

retrieved_docs.title: Tardive Dyskinesia -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Data on the management of tardive dyskinesia are inconsistent and difficult to understand. Some studies show an improvement when the antipsychotic drug is decreased in dose or discontinued, and other studies show no change. Long-term studies suggest that tardive dyskinesia persists in at least 10% to 30% of patients. Recent studies show that the outcomes of tardive dyskinesia are improved in younger patients treated with low doses for shorter periods. Switching to low potency antipsychotic drugs has been shown to lower the risk of tardive dyskinesia. Recently, the FDA approved the drug valbenazine to treat tardive dyskinesia. Early data from the clinical trial reveals that the drug effectively alleviates tardive dyskinesia and is safe. However, the study was conducted by many physicians who also received some type of compensation from the pharmaceutical companies, so one has to take this data with a grain of salt until more long-term data are available. [2] [13] [Level 5]

retrieved_docs.title: Extrapyramidal Side Effects -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Extrapyramidal side effects, sometimes referred to as medication-induced movement disorders, are significant adverse effects typically caused by antipsychotic medications and other dopamine receptor-blocking agents. While centrally acting dopamine receptor-blocking agents, such as antipsychotic medications, are the most common medications associated with extrapyramidal side effects, almost all antipsychotic medications can cause one or more of these effects in a dose-dependent manner. Acute manifestations of these adverse effects include but are not limited to dystonia, akathisia, tremor, and parkinsonism; typical chronic manifestations include tardive dyskinesia, tardive akathisia, and tardive dystonia. Extrapyramidal side effects can cause stigma and distress in patients and are associated with treatment nonadherence.

retrieved_docs.title: Tardive Dyskinesia -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Tardive dyskinesia is caused due to long-term exposure to first and second-generation neuroleptics, certain antidepressants, lithium, and some antiemetic medications. Typically, first-generation antipsychotics with increased dopamine D2 receptor affinity are affiliated with a higher risk of inducing tardive dyskinesia. Atypical antipsychotics have been associated with a decreased tardive dyskinesia diathesis--as well as fewer extrapyramidal side effects compared to typical antipsychotics--due to their lower affinity for dopamine D2 receptors in the dorsal striatum and concomitant blockade of serotonin 5-HT2A/2C receptors. [4] [5] Metoclopramide can also cause tardive dyskinesia, especially in older individuals. The condition may also occur with certain antihistamines, amoxapine, and fluoxetine.

retrieved_docs.title: Extrapyramidal Side Effects -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Tardive dyskinesia and other tardive syndromes are treated by gradually tapering or discontinuing the causative medication. There may be transient worsening of symptoms during withdrawal of the causative medication. Anticholinergic medications and antiparkinsonian medications should be discontinued, as they may worsen tardive dyskinesia. [47] Patients may be switched to second-generation antipsychotic medications such as quetiapine or clozapine with a lower risk of tardive movements. Dopamine-depleting medications known as vesicular monoamine transporter 2 (VMAT2) inhibitors are the first medications approved by the Food and Drug Administration to treat tardive syndromes. These medications include valbenazine and deutetrabenazine. [48] Tetrabenazine was a VMAT2 inhibitor approved for dyskinesias in Huntington's disease and has been used off-label for tardive syndromes. [49] [50]

retrieved_docs.title: Tardive Dyskinesia -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Movement disorders like tardive dyskinesias are frequently aggravated by the use of drugs that block dopamine. In susceptible patients, even a single dose of an anti-dopaminergic drug can quickly develop disabling movement disorders. Thus, besides the clinician, the role of the pharmacist is critical. Patients vulnerable to tardive dyskinesia include people with schizophrenia and those with developmental disabilities who may develop the movement disorder when administered a dopamine blocking agent. The pharmacist must note these patients and with which medications they are treated. The caregiver should be informed about the possibility of this adverse effect and the alternatives. Nurses can also monitor the patients for symptomatic improvement or decline and immediately report their observations to the prescriber and/or pharmacist to enact appropriate therapeutic interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: Tardive Dyskinesia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: If tardive dyskinesia is present in conjunction with dementia, one should consider Huntington disease, Wilson disease, or a central nervous system tumor. A diagnosis of antipsychotic-induced tardive dyskinesia is made after the symptoms have persisted for at least one month and required exposure to neuroleptics for at least three months. Tardive dyskinesia should be distinguished from withdrawal dyskinesias as these most likely remit soon after the termination of the antipsychotic.

retrieved_docs.title: Tardive Dyskinesia -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: The best means of obviating tardive dyskinesia is primary prevention. Primary prevention of tardive dyskinesia includes using the lowest effective dose of an antipsychotic agent for the shortest period possible. If tardive dyskinesia develops, it is recommended to decrease the dose or even discontinue the offending agent and switch to clozapine. Clozapine is recommended in patients with tardive dyskinesia who continue to require antipsychotics, as the incidence of tardive dyskinesia with clozapine is significantly less than with other antipsychotics.

retrieved_docs.title: Extrapyramidal Side Effects -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Extrapyramidal side effects, also referred to as medication-induced movement disorders, are common adverse drug reactions caused by antipsychotic medications and other dopamine D2 receptor-blocking agents. These effects were first described in 1952 after chlorpromazine was recognized to produce side effects resembling Parkinson disease. [1] Acute manifestations include dystonia, akathisia, tremor, and parkinsonism, whereas chronic manifestations include tardive dyskinesia, tardive akathisia, and tardive dystonia. Extrapyramidal symptoms may be hyperkinetic or hypokinetic (parkinsonian) movement disorders, with varying pathophysiology. [2] These symptoms may arise due to medication use, but they can also occur without any medication exposure. Extrapyramidal symptoms are debilitating and interfere with social functioning and communication, motor tasks, and activities of daily living. As a result, individuals may experience an impaired quality of life, treatment nonadherence, increased morbidity, caregiver burden, and increased utilization of healthcare resources. [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Tardive Dystonia -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: One study attempted to demarcate outcome groups based upon symptom resolution following treatment. In this study of 107 patients being treated for tardive dystonia, 14% were in remission, 39% were improved, and 50% noted either no improvement or exacerbating symptomatology. [4] Within this cohort, those who discontinued their neuroleptic were more likely to have experienced symptom remission. More specifically, those who discontinued antipsychotics more rapidly benefitted from a higher likelihood of remission. [4] Remission was reached within a mean of 2.4 years from the discontinuation of all neuroleptic agents. [4] Furthermore, dystonia appears irreversible in those exposed to neuroleptics for greater than 10 years.

retrieved_docs.title: Tardive Dyskinesia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Tardive dyskinesia (TD) is a syndrome that encompasses a constellation of iatrogenic movement disorders caused by the antagonism of dopamine receptors. The movement disorders include akathisia, dystonia, buccolingual stereotypy, chorea, tics, and other abnormal involuntary movements. Most often, these dyskinetic disorders precipitate following chronic antipsychotic administration; however, several other medications are also associated with tardive dyskinesia. The Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, Fifth Edition (DSM 5) defines tardive dyskinesia as a medication-induced movement disorder that persists despite discontinuation or change of the medications. As per DSM-5, to confirm a diagnosis of tardive dyskinesia, symptoms must persist for at least one month after discontinuation of the medication. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Tardive Dyskinesia -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnosis and management of tardive dyskinesia are best made with an interprofessional team. In most cases, the primary clinician may suspect the diagnosis during follow-up. It is crucial to consult collaboratively with a pharmacist as part of the interprofessional team approach to managing the medication profile for patients who have developed tardive dyskinesia or are at risk for developing the condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Tardive Dystonia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Tardive dystonia (TD) is one of the extrapyramidal syndromes caused by the use of dopaminergic receptor antagonists. If not recognized promptly and appropriately managed, symptoms can worsen or remain indefinitely. This activity outlines the evaluation and management of tardive dystonia and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in providing care for patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Haloperidol -- Contraindications -- Tardive dyskinesia ( TD)
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic antipsychotic medicines should primarily be considered for patients with chronic illnesses responsive to such treatments. In addition, these medications should be considered for patients who lack access to alternative or equally effective, potentially less toxic treatments. When chronic treatment is necessary, clinicians should recommend using the smallest effective dose and the shortest feasible treatment duration that achieves a reasonable clinical response.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1574
retrieved_docs.content: Antipsychotics are linked to خلل الحركة المتأخر, a late‑onset movement disorder (commonly known as tardive dyskinesia) that can develop after prolonged use of these medications; recognizing this relationship is essential for clinicians to monitor patients on antipsychotic therapy for abnormal involuntary movements and to adjust treatment strategies to reduce the risk of this adverse effect.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 1184
retrieved_docs.content: Antipsychotics are a class of psychotropic medications used to manage conditions such as schizophrenia and bipolar disorder, and they are distinct from ondansetron, which is a serotonin 5‑HT₃ receptor antagonist employed primarily to prevent nausea and vomiting. A recognized long‑term adverse effect of antipsychotic therapy is the development of tardive movement disorders, referred to in Arabic as “خلل الحركة المتأخر.” This connection highlights the need for careful monitoring of patients receiving antipsychotics for extrapyramidal side effects and differentiating these agents from unrelated drugs like ondansetron.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1475
retrieved_docs.content: Methotrimeprazine, acepromazine, and pomaglumetad methionil are all members of the antipsychotic drug class. As typical antipsychotics, these agents are used to manage psychotic disorders by modulating neurotransmitter pathways, particularly dopamine signaling. A notable clinical consideration for antipsychotics, including methotrimeprazine, acepromazine, and pomaglumetad methionil, is their association with late‑onset movement abnormalities, such as tardive dyskinesia (referred to in Arabic as “خلل الحركة المتأخر”). Awareness of this potential adverse effect is essential for clinicians when prescribing and monitoring patients receiving these antipsychotic medications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 475
retrieved_docs.content: Imidazole is a heterocyclic chemical scaffold that serves as a parent structure for several therapeutic agents, most notably the antifungal medication clotrimazole, which is an imidazole derivative. Although ondansetron is sometimes mentioned in connection with the imidazole scaffold, it does not belong to the imidazole class; ondansetron is a serotonin 5‑HT₃‑receptor antagonist primarily used as an antiemetic. Antipsychotics constitute a distinct pharmacological class employed to manage psychotic disorders, and they are not a subtype of ondansetron. A well‑recognized adverse effect of long‑term antipsychotic use is “خلل الحركة المتأخر,” the Arabic term for tardive dyskinesia, a late‑onset movement disorder. These relationships underscore the need to differentiate chemical parent structures from therapeutic categories and to remain vigilant for movement‑related side effects in patients receiving antipsychotic therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 442
retrieved_docs.content: Antipsychotics constitute a drug class that includes agents such as methotrimeprazine, acepromazine and pomaglumetad methionil. These medications are linked to substances that depress central nervous system (CNS) activity and are therefore related to CNS‑depressant agents. In clinical practice, antipsychotics are also associated with the development of late‑onset movement disorders, commonly referred to as tardive dyskinesia. The broader category of physiological effects of drugs comprises several subgroups, including CNS‑depressant substances, hypoglycemic agents, hypnotics and sedatives, and CNS stimulants. Thus, antipsychotics intersect with the CNS‑depressant subgroup of drug effects while also carrying a risk of movement‑related adverse effects, highlighting the importance of careful selection and monitoring in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1417
retrieved_docs.content: Benperidol, chlorpromazine, levomepromazine maleate, methotrimeprazine hydrochloride, and prochlorperazine mesylate are all medications that may be used to treat اضطراب ذُهاني (psychotic disorder). These agents, which belong to the class of typical antipsychotics, share the therapeutic purpose of alleviating the symptoms of psychosis, such as delusions, hallucinations, and thought disorganization. Their use in clinical practice reflects the broader strategy of employing dopamine‑blocking drugs to manage psychotic conditions, and selecting among them can be guided by factors such as side‑effect profile, patient tolerance, and specific clinical presentation. Effective treatment with any of these drugs can improve patient outcomes by reducing psychotic symptoms and enhancing functional stability.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1353
retrieved_docs.content: Promazine is classified as a dopamine antagonist, meaning it belongs to the group of agents that block dopamine receptors. As a member of this pharmacologic class, promazine exerts its effects by inhibiting dopaminergic neurotransmission, a mechanism commonly utilized in the treatment of psychiatric disorders such as schizophrenia and in managing certain types of nausea. Understanding that promazine functions as a dopamine antagonist helps clinicians anticipate its therapeutic actions as well as potential side effects related to dopamine blockade, such as extrapyramidal symptoms, sedation, and anticholinergic effects. This classification informs appropriate patient care decisions, including drug selection, dosing, and monitoring for adverse reactions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 50
retrieved_docs.content: drugs belong to multiple therapeutic categories—psychostimulants, nootropics, anti‑dementia agents, antidepressants, mood stabilizers, and antipsychotics—and recognizing their shared mechanisms, subclass memberships, and contraindications is essential for safe and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 1558
retrieved_docs.content: Promazine is classified both as an antiemetic and as a dopamine antagonist. As an antiemetic, promazine is used to prevent or treat nausea and vomiting, while its role as a dopamine antagonist means it blocks dopamine receptors, a mechanism that contributes to its anti‑nausea effects and can also be relevant in managing conditions such as psychosis or movement disorders. The overlap between these categories highlights that many dopamine‑blocking agents, including promazine, possess antiemetic properties, which is important for clinicians when selecting appropriate therapy for patients who require control of nausea or benefit from dopamine receptor inhibition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 627
retrieved_docs.content: Bromocriptine mesylate, bromodiphenhydramine, chlormethiazol, kabergolin, pergolid, pimavanserin, piribedil, pramipexol and tolcapon are all agents that may be used to treat Parkinson’s disease, indicating a range of pharmacologic options for managing the motor and non‑motor symptoms of this neurodegenerative disorder. Clinically, Parkinson’s disease is also associated with depressive disorders (اضطرابات اكتئابية) and tremor (رعاش), highlighting the importance of addressing both psychiatric and movement‑related manifestations in patient care. Recognizing these therapeutic possibilities and associated clinical features supports a comprehensive approach to the diagnosis, treatment, and ongoing management of individuals with Parkinson’s disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2595
retrieved_docs.content: Benperidol, chlorpromazine, levomepromazine maleate, prochlorperazine mesylate, and trifluoperazine are all pharmacologic agents that may be used to treat اضطراب ذُهاني (psychotic disorder). These drugs belong to the class of typical antipsychotics and exert their therapeutic effect primarily through antagonism of dopamine D₂ receptors, thereby reducing the positive symptoms of psychosis such as delusions and hallucinations. Chlorpromazine, the first widely used antipsychotic, and its related compounds levomepromazine maleate and prochlorperazine mesylate share similar mechanisms and are employed in both acute and chronic management of psychotic states. Benperidol and trifluoperazine, also dopamine antagonists, are utilized when additional potency or specific side‑effect profiles are desired. The inclusion of these agents in treatment regimens underscores the importance of selecting an appropriate antipsychotic based on efficacy, tolerability, and patient‑specific factors to achieve optimal control of psychotic symptoms and improve overall patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1977
retrieved_docs.content: Bromocriptine mesylate, chlormethiazol, and kabergolin are pharmacologic agents that may be used to treat Parkinson’s disease. Parkinson’s disease is clinically associated with اضطرابات اكتئابية (depressive disorders) and رعاش (tremor), reflecting its impact on both mood and motor function. In addition, Parkinson’s disease frequently co‑occurs with حالة نفسية غير طبيعية (abnormal mental states), underscoring the importance of comprehensive assessment and management of both neurological and psychiatric aspects in affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2145
retrieved_docs.content: Bromodiphenhydramine and pimavanserin are pharmacologic agents that may be employed in the treatment of Parkinson’s disease. Bromodiphenhydramine, an antihistamine with anticholinergic activity, has potential utility for alleviating motor symptoms associated with the neurodegenerative disorder. Pimavanserin, a selective serotonin‑5‑HT₂A inverse agonist, is also recognized for its possible therapeutic role in Parkinson’s disease, particularly for managing psychotic features and may provide broader symptomatic benefit. Recognizing both drugs as possible treatment options expands therapeutic considerations for clinicians managing patients with Parkinson’s disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 90
retrieved_docs.content: Bromocriptine mesylate, bromodiphenhydramine, chlormethiazol, kabergolin, pergolid, piribedil, pramipexol, and tolcapon are all agents that may be used to treat Parkinson’s disease. In clinical practice, Parkinson’s disease is frequently associated with اضطرابات اكتئابية (depressive disorders) and رعاش (tremor), highlighting the importance of addressing both motor and non‑motor symptoms when selecting therapeutic strategies. The inclusion of multiple pharmacologic options reflects the heterogeneous nature of Parkinson’s disease and the need for individualized treatment plans that can alleviate tremor, improve motor function, and potentially mitigate associated depressive symptoms.

Parkinson’s disease is clinically associated with اضطرابات اكتئابية (depressive disorders) and رعاش (tremor). Recognizing both the potential therapeutic options and the motor and non‑motor clinical associations underscores the importance of comprehensive management for patients with Parkinson’s disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1396
retrieved_docs.content: Prochlorperazine edisylate and chlorpromazine hydrochloride are both classified as phenothiazine compounds, indicating that they belong to the same chemical class of antipsychotic and antiemetic agents. This shared classification reflects common pharmacologic properties inherent to phenothiazine drugs, such as dopamine receptor antagonism, which underlies their therapeutic use in managing psychosis, nausea, and related conditions. Recognizing that both medications are phenothiazines helps clinicians understand their similar mechanisms of action, potential side effect profiles, and considerations for patient care when selecting or monitoring treatment within this drug class.
